reasoning: The sink in dp_redirect() (duplicatepage.php lines 379-388) is correctly identified as technically defective: it applies esc_url_raw() to $url and concatenates the result into a JavaScript string literal via wp_add_inline_script, using window.location.href="$web_url"; — esc_url_raw does not encode double-quotes, so a URL with a literal double-quote would break out of the JS string. However, no attacker-controlled input reaches this sink in current code. Both call sites are in inc/admin-settings.php lines 23-25 and both pass compile-time hardcoded string literals ('options-general.php?page=duplicate_page_settings&msg=1' and '...&msg=2'). These strings contain no double-quotes and no user-supplied data. The entire code path is also triple-gated behind manage_options (WordPress administrator): (1) add_options_page registers the page with manage_options capability, (2) duplicate_page_settings() checks current_user_can('manage_options') before including admin-settings.php, (3) admin-settings.php line 12 checks current_user_can('manage_options') && wp_verify_nonce(...) before calling dp_redirect(). There is no exploitable path with current code. The function is a latent/future risk if a new caller introduces user input, but as-is this is not a real exploitable vulnerability.
